title: Falko Jüßen
text:
Falko Jüßen





Lehrstuhl für Internationale Wirtschaft und Regionalökonomik
Schumpeter School of Business and Economics
University of Wuppertal

Contact University Page

title: Falko Jüßen
text:
Short Bio
Falko Jüßen is Professor of International Economics and Regional Economics at the Schumpeter School of Business and Economics, University of Wuppertal. Since 2016, he has served as Vice Dean of the Schumpeter School of Business and Economics. His research focuses on family economics, labor economics, and macroeconomics, using theoretical, empirical, and quantitative methods. He received his doctorate and Diplom in economics from TU Dortmund and held academic positions at TU Dortmund and the University of Bonn before joining Wuppertal in 2013. He contributed to the DFG Collaborative Research Center SFB 823, “Household Economics, Labor Markets, and Macroeconomic Dynamics,” and has held several research and teaching visits at the University of Zurich. He is an IZA Research Fellow, and his work has appeared in journals including the Journal of Labor Economics, American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics, European Economic Review, and the Journal of Money, Credit and Banking.

title: Research
text:
My current research focuses on commitment problems within households. I study how dynamic household decisions are shaped by the fact that family members cannot always commit today to future actions or future sharing rules. This matters, for example, when spouses respond to income shocks, decide how much to save, insure each other within the family, or renegotiate household arrangements when outside options change.
A central theme is limited commitment: household members may cooperate as long as doing so remains attractive, but bargaining positions can shift over time and, in some cases, household dissolution or divorce becomes the relevant outside option. I use theoretical, quantitative, and empirical methods to analyze how these forces affect labor supply, saving behavior, risk sharing, and the design of public policies such as social insurance, taxation, and divorce law.


Family Economics
Labor Economics
Macroeconomics
Theoretical, Empirical, and Quantitative Methods







DFG Research Grant, joint project with Christian Bredemeier.
Project no. 516317370. Grant references: JU 3247/1-1 and BR 7012/1-1.
Funding: DFG Research Grant, approx. EUR 250,000 direct project funding.
Term: since 2023 / 36 months.
The project studies how limited commitment within households shapes family insurance, saving behavior, and the economic consequences of divorce law. It combines quantitative dynamic household models with empirical analysis of panel microdata, including individual life-satisfaction measures, to reassess policy questions in social insurance, taxation, and divorce law.
DFG GEPRIS project page






Recommended version

Household Chores, Taxes, and the Labor-Supply Elasticities of Women and Men. With Dorothée Bahn and Christian Bredemeier. Revised manuscript, June 2026.

Please use the June 2026 version below. An earlier version appeared as Ruhr Economic Papers, No. 1177, RWI - Leibniz-Institut für Wirtschaftsforschung, Essen, 2025.


Latest version (June 2026) EconStor record Earlier REP PDF

title: Teaching
text:
My teaching covers international economics, macroeconomics, labor markets, and quantitative methods. Courses have been taught at the undergraduate, graduate, and doctoral level.
